fr:mainmatter.fr:p: If B is a retract of A and A is a proposition, then B is a proposition.
This can be seen using the cancellation laws of retractions.
If a type A is contractible, then it is a proposition.
As A is contractible with some centre a:A, we have for any x,y:A identifications \chi _x \colon a = x and \chi _y \colon a = y. Therefore, we identify x with y using \chi _x ^{-1} \cdot \chi _y.
If a type A is a proposition, then it is a set.
If A is a proposition, then for all x,y:A the identification type x=y is contractible.
The centre of contraction of x=y is given by our assumption that A is a proposition; the contracting identifications are obtained from the same assumption via .
If A is n+1-truncated, then for each x,y:A the identification type x=y is n-truncated.
By induction on n; the base case for n=-2 is given by , and the other cases are trivial.
If a type A is n-truncated, then it is also n+1-truncated.
By induction on the truncation level n, with two base cases given by and .
For any type A, the identity map 1_{ A } \colon A \to A is contractible.
Immediate by identification induction.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The identity map is n-truncated for all n

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: For any type A, the identity map 1_{ A } \colon A \to A is n-truncated for all n.

We proceed by induction on the truncation level n: when n=-2, we recall that the identity map is contractible, and in the inductive step we apply the upward closure of n-truncation to each fiber of 1_{ A }.
If B is a retract of A and A is a proposition, then B is a proposition.
This can be seen using the cancellation laws of retractions.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Contractible types are closed under retracts

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: If B is a retract of A and A is contractible, then B is a contractible.
Let s \colon B \to A be a section of r \colon A \to B; if a_0 is the centre of contraction of A, then the centre of contraction of B is given by ra_0. For the rest, we use and .

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: n-truncated types are closed under retracts

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Any retract of an n-truncated type is n-truncated.

By induction on n; the base cases for n=-2 and n=-1 are given by and respectively. In the inductive step, we assume that s \colon B \to A exhibits B as a retract of A and that for each x,y:A the identification type x=_Ay is n+1-truncated to show that for all u,v:B the identification type u=_Bv is likewise n+1-truncated. The result follows from our assumption and the fact that u=_Bv is (by ) a retract of su=_Asv.
When s \colon A \to B is a section of r \colon B \to A, we say that A is a retract of B.
A type A is said to be a proposition when every x:A, the type A is contractible with centre x, i.e. all elements of A are identified.
For any function f \colon A \to B, the type of inverses to f is a proposition.

Without loss of generality, we may assume that f has an inverse \mathopen {} \left ( g, \epsilon , \eta , \chi \right ) \mathclose {}. By , it suffices to argue that the type of inverses to f is a retract of a proposition; the type of inverses to f is in fact equivalent to the type of sections equipped with a coherent remainder:

By , it suffices to show that the type of sections to f is a proposition and that for every such section, the coherent remainder thereof is a proposition. The latter is , and the former follows from by means of the retraction \mathopen {} \left ( g, \eta \right ) \mathclose {}.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The coherent remainder of a section is a proposition

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \sigma \equiv \mathopen {} \left ( g \colon B \to A, \epsilon \colon f \circ g = 1_{ B } \right ) \mathclose {} be a section of a function f \colon A \to B. Then the coherent remainder of \sigma is a proposition.
By , we may argue that \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{ f } \sigma is a retract of a proposition. We let P be the following type defined using path object structure of fibers given in :
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} P &: \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathopen {} \left ( g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} , \epsilon \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathsf {fiber} \, f \, \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} } \mathopen {} \left ( x, \mathsf {refl} \right ) \mathclose {} \\ P & \equiv \mathchoice { \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \prod _{ \mathopen {} \left ( x:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} = x \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} = x \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} = x \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( p : g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} = x \right ) \mathclose {} } } \epsilon \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} = \mathsf {act}_{ f } p \cdot \mathsf {refl} \end {aligned}
fr:mainmatter.fr:p: It is not difficult to show using that \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{ f } \sigma is a retract of P; we will not need the further fact that it is equivalent to P.

Therefore, it remains to show that P is a proposition. Without loss of generality, we may assume that we have some h: P. By , we may fix x:A and prove that \mathopen {} \left ( g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} , \epsilon \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} \cong _{ \mathsf {fiber} \, f \, \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} } \mathopen {} \left ( x, \mathsf {refl} \right ) \mathclose {} is a proposition. Of course, this is a retract of the identification type \mathopen {} \left ( g \mathopen {} \left ( f x \right ) \mathclose {} , \epsilon \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} =_{ \mathsf {fiber} \, f \, \mathopen {} \left ( fx \right ) \mathclose {} } \mathopen {} \left ( x, \mathsf {refl} \right ) \mathclose {} and so by it is enough to prove that the latter is a proposition. In fact, we will deduce this via by observing that the fiber of f at fx is contractible: using our assumption h:P and the distributivity of products over sums, we may construct a retraction of f exhibiting f as biinvertible and thus by contractible.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: n-truncated types are closed under retracts

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Any retract of an n-truncated type is n-truncated.

By induction on n; the base cases for n=-2 and n=-1 are given by and respectively. In the inductive step, we assume that s \colon B \to A exhibits B as a retract of A and that for each x,y:A the identification type x=_Ay is n+1-truncated to show that for all u,v:B the identification type u=_Bv is likewise n+1-truncated. The result follows from our assumption and the fact that u=_Bv is (by ) a retract of su=_Asv.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Display of right-cloven families of path objects

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {A} be a path object. Any right-cloven family of path objects \mathcal {B} \colon \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} \to \mathsf {PathObj} gives rise to a canonical displayed path object over \mathcal {A} in which:

fr:ol.fr:li: for p \colon x \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y, we define b \approx _{ \mathcal {B} }^{ p } b' to be b \approx _{ \mathcal {B} y } p^*b';

for x: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} and b: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} x \right \rvert \mathclose {}, the reflexivity datum \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {B} }^{ x } {b} \colon b \approx _{ \mathcal {B} }^{ \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {x} } b is given by the component \Phi _{ \mathcal {B} }xb \colon b \approx _{ \mathcal {B} x } \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {x}^*b of the unit law of the given cleaving of \mathcal {B}.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Univalence is more difficult to establish than for the case of left cleavings (). Fixing a: \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {}, we must show that the reflexive graph structure on \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} a \right \rvert \mathclose {} with edges in b \approx _{ \mathcal {B} a } \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {a}^*b' and reflexivity data for b:Ba given by \Phi _B ab \colon b \approx _{ \mathcal {B} a } \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {a}^*b is univalent.

It suffices to show that the type of singletons \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( b': \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} a \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( b': \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} a \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( b': \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} a \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( b': \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} a \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } } b \approx _{ \mathcal {B} a } \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } {a}^*b' is a proposition. As each \mathcal {B} a is univalent, this is easily shown to be a retract of \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( b': \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} a \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( b': \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} a \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( b': \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} a \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( b': \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {B} a \right \rvert \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} } } b \approx _{ \mathcal {B} a } b', which is a proposition again by the univalence of \mathcal {B} a. Therefore, we conclude via .
Let f \colon A \to B be a section of some r \colon B \to A; the type of sections of f is, then, a proposition.

Using the univalence principle for dependent function spaces, we can see that the type of sections of f \colon A \to B is a retract of the fiber of f \circ - at 1_{ B }, using the univalence principle for sections.

To show that the type of sections of f is a proposition, we may assume beforehand that we have such a section of f; therefore, because we have assumed a retraction of f already, we know that f is biinvertible and therefore that f is contractible. By , it suffices to show that the fiber of f \circ - at 1_{ B } is a proposition, which follows via and .

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Contractible types are closed under retracts

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: If B is a retract of A and A is contractible, then B is a contractible.
Let s \colon B \to A be a section of r \colon A \to B; if a_0 is the centre of contraction of A, then the centre of contraction of B is given by ra_0. For the rest, we use and .

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Restriction of path objects along quasi-inverses

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {A} be a path object and let B be a type. Given a quasi-inverse function g \colon B \to \mathopen {} \left \lvert \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {}, we can restrict the path object \mathcal {A} to a path object structure g^* \mathcal {A} on B as follows:
fr:mainmatter.fr:tex: \begin {aligned} \mathopen {} \left \lvert g^* \mathcal {A} \right \rvert \mathclose {} &: \equiv B \\ x \approx _{ g^* \mathcal {A} } y &: \equiv gx \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } gy \\ \mathsf {rx}_{ g^* \mathcal {A} } x &: \equiv \mathsf {rx}_{ \mathcal {A} } \mathopen {} \left ( gx \right ) \mathclose {} \end {aligned}

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Fixing x:B, we must show that \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:B \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:B \right ) \mathclose {} } } gx \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } gy is a proposition. But this is a retract of \mathchoice { \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \textstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } }{ \scriptscriptstyle \sum _{ \mathopen {} \left ( y:A \right ) \mathclose {} } } gx \approx _{ \mathcal {A} } y, which is a proposition by the univalence of \mathcal {A}; thus the result follows from the closure of propositions under retracts ().
